title: Eris progress
text:
Welcome to the Eris progress page! Here, I will be sharing regular updates on my work and progress on the Eris project. Stay tuned for insights, milestones, and reflections as I continue to develop and enhance Eris.
Any questions please reach out to me!

title: Progress report week 3
text:
Which modules has mappy:

Which modules inherited the mappy:

title: Progress report week 3
text:
Minimap2
Let’s run the mappy without any eris and see what is the result.
After reading the minimap2 API, it seems minimap2 required tuning for specific data type (i.e., differ between long vs short reads, ONT vs pac-bio)

import mappy as mp
import pandas as pd

# Obtain the query first 
# !bedtools getfasta -fi data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta \
#     -bed data/complete_ec958_IS.reanotated.sorted.bed -s -name > data/IS_elements.fa

# Path
comp_fa_path = "../../data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta"

# Align the complete genome assembly fasta file
comp_fa_aln = mp.Aligner(comp_fa_path)

#Read IS sequences and map them
with open("../result/minimap2_IS.bed", "w") as bed:
    for is_name, is_seq, _ in mp.fastx_read("../../data/IS_n.mmi"):
        for hit in comp_fa_aln.map(is_seq):
            print(hit)
            if not hit.is_primary:
                continue
            strand = "+" if hit.strand == 1 else "-"
            bed.write(f"{hit.ctg}\t{hit.r_st}\t{hit.r_en}\t{is_name}\t{hit.mapq}\t{strand}\n")
print("It's done!")

It's done!


Nothing hit want to try other way
# Map this to the query
# minimap2 -c --cs -x asm5 data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta data/IS_elements.fa > progress/result/complete_map_IS.paf

# # Indexing
# minimap2 -d data/IS.mmi data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta 


import pandas as pd

rows = []
with open("../result/minimap2/complete_map_IS.paf") as f:
    for line in f:
        cols = line.strip().split("\t")
        q_name  = cols[0]
        t_name  = cols[5]
        t_st    = int(cols[7])
        t_en    = int(cols[8])
        strand  = cols[4]
        mapq    = int(cols[11])
        rows.append([t_name, t_st, t_en, q_name, mapq, strand])

total_is_file = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["transcript","start","end","name","score","strand"])
total_is_file.to_csv("../result/complete_map_IS.bed", sep="\t", index=False, header=False)
print(total_is_file.head(10))
print(total_is_file.shape)

title: Progress report week 1
text: Input data: - Complete genome assembly: obtain from Genbank (contains chromosome and plasmids) - Shovill simulated: only the chromosome - The realfastq simulated: only the chromosome - .mmi the genome pathway - .gfa is the assembly graph

title: Progress report week 1
section: Convert the Fasta into GFA format
text:
Convert the Fasta into GFA format
Since the complete genome assembly is in .fasta format, we need to convert it into .gfa format to run Eris. The .gfa format has an important information where we can see the overlaps, paths, and coverage, while the FASTA lack of this information.
We used minigraph to convert the fasta file into gfa format. The command is as follows:
# minigraph -xggs data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta > data/EC958_complete_genome.gfa

# rerun Eris with the new gfa file
# uv run eris -i ../data/EC958_complete_genome.gfa -d ../data/IS.mmi -o ../result/complete_gfa/eris_complete_gfa
Weakness of this might be not real topological information of the genome assembly, as the gfa file is generated from the fasta file.

# Load the new output file
complete_gfa_df = pd.read_csv("../result/com_gfa/eris_complete_gfa_report.tsv", sep="\t")

# Check the dimension of the new dataframe
print("Dimension of the new dataframe:", complete_gfa_df.shape)
# Group IS elements together and count the number of occurrences for each IS element in the new dataframe
complete_gfa_counts = complete_gfa_df.groupby("target")["target"].count()
print("Total IS element counts in the new dataframe:", complete_gfa_counts.count())

Dimension of the new dataframe: (37, 9)
Total IS element counts in the new dataframe: 4


Turns out that there are still only 4 IS found in the complete genome assembly after converting it into gfa format.

title: Progress report week 1
section: Fragmentation of the complete genome assembly from the fasta file
text:
Fragmentation of the complete genome assembly from the fasta file
I want to try create a fragmentations of the complete genome assembly to see if it can improve the results. As the complete genome assembly has 3 sequences, I will try to create 150 fragments for each sequence.

# Create fragments of the complete genome assembly
from Bio import SeqIO
from Bio.SeqRecord import SeqRecord
import random

complete_fasta = "../../data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta"
fragmented_fasta = "../../data/EC958_complete_genome_fragmented.fasta"

def fragment_genome(input_fasta: str, output_fasta: str, num_fragments: int = 150):
    records = list(SeqIO.parse(input_fasta, "fasta"))
    fragments = []
    
    for record in records:
        seq_length = len(record.seq)
        fragment_size = seq_length // num_fragments
        
        for i in range(num_fragments):
            start = i * fragment_size
            end = (i + 1) * fragment_size if i < num_fragments - 1 else seq_length
            fragment_seq = record.seq[start:end]
            fragment_id = f"{record.id}_fragment_{i+1}"
            fragments.append(SeqRecord(fragment_seq, id=fragment_id, description=""))
    
    SeqIO.write(fragments, output_fasta, "fasta")
    print(f"Written {len(fragments)} fragments to {output_fasta}")


# Fragment the complete genome assembly
fragment_genome(complete_fasta, 
    fragmented_fasta, 
    num_fragments=150)

Written 450 fragments to ../../data/EC958_complete_genome_fragmented.fasta


After creating the fragmented fasta file, we can convert it into gfa format and run Eris again to see if there are more IS elements found in the complete genome assembly.
# Try to use Eris with the fragmented complete genome assembly
# uv run eris --genome ../data/EC958_complete_genome_fragmented.fasta -d ../data/IS.mmi -o ../result/complete_fragmented/eris_complete_fragmented -q

Investigate the output file after fragmentation

# Load the new output file after fragmentation
import pandas as pd
complete_fragmented_df = pd.read_csv("../result/com_frag/eris_complete_fragmented_report.tsv", sep="\t")
# Check the dimension of the new dataframe after fragmentation  
print("Dimension of the new dataframe after fragmentation:", complete_fragmented_df.shape)
# Group IS elements together and count the number of occurrences for each IS element in the new dataframe
complete_fragmented_counts = complete_fragmented_df.groupby("target")["target"].count()
print("Total IS element counts in the new dataframe after fragmentation:", complete_fragmented_counts.count())

Dimension of the new dataframe after fragmentation: (212, 9)
Total IS element counts in the new dataframe after fragmentation: 24


There are more IS found in the fragmented genome. However, we want to know whether is it the same IS found in the simulated data. Otherwise also report, the one that not found in the simulated data.

# Check if the IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly are also found in the simulated
common_fragmented = set(complete_fragmented_counts.index) & set(simulated_counts.index)
print("Number of common IS elements found in both datasets after fragmentation:", len(common_fragmented))
print("IS elements found in both datasets after fragmentation:", common_fragmented)

# Check the IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly that are not found in the simulated data
unique_fragmented = set(complete_fragmented_counts.index) - set(simulated_counts.index)
print("Number of IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly that are not found in the simulated data:", len(unique_fragmented))
print("IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly that are not found in the simulated data:", unique_fragmented)

title: Progress report week 2
text:
Total disruption

def calc_disruption(df):
    total = int((df['effect'] == 'DISRUPTED').sum())
    percentage = float(total / len(df) *100)
    return total, round(percentage, 3)

print("Total IS that is disrupted in complete genome:", calc_disruption(complete_df))
print("Total IS that is disrupted in fragmented genome:", calc_disruption(complete_fragmented_df))
print("Total IS that is disrupted in simulated genome:", calc_disruption(simulated_df))

Total IS that is disrupted in complete genome: (7, 18.919)
Total IS that is disrupted in fragmented genome: (24, 11.321)
Total IS that is disrupted in simulated genome: (16, 5.461)

title: Progress report week 2
section: Calculate it based on the names
text:
Calculate it based on the names

# Make a list for the name only and separate the underscore 
# We remove any types in different position
ref_list = list(ann_bed["name"].str.split('_').str[0].drop_duplicates())

print(f'Total IS found in the list{len(ref_list)}')
print(ref_list)

ref_set = set(ref_list)

# Flag each row whether it's found or not
clean_frag['correct'] = clean_frag['Name'].isin(ref_set)

# Overall accuracy
accuracy_frag = clean_frag['correct'].mean()
print(f'Accuracy is {accuracy_frag:.2%}')

# Names not predicted in the list
wrong_frag = clean_frag.loc[~clean_frag['correct'], 'Name'].unique()
print("Names not found in reference list:", wrong_frag)

Total IS found in the list38

title: Progress report week 2
text:
Analysing IS15

# Extract only the rows with IS15 on it
is15_df = complete_fragmented_df[complete_fragmented_df['target'].str.startswith("IS15")]
is15_df = is15_df.copy()

parts = is15_df['gene_id'].str.split('_')

# Extract the location
is15_df['transcript'] = parts.str[0:3].str.join('_')
is15_df['start'] = parts.str[3]
is15_df['end'] = parts.str[4]
is15_df['score'] = 0
is15_df['name'] = is15_df['target'].str.split('_').str[0]
# Map the strand
is15_df['strand'] = is15_df['orientation'].map({'same strand': '+', 'opposite strand': '-'})

print(is15_df.head(10))

# Create new bedfile
is15_bed = is15_df[['transcript', 'start', 'end', 'name','score', 'strand']]
is15_bed.to_csv('../../data/IS15_data.bed', sep="\t", header=False, index=False)

title: Progress report week 4
text:
The complete genome assembly has more IS found compared to the .gfa file after sorting it.

from Bio import SeqIO
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path

def unique_is(filepath: str):
    df = pd.read_table(filepath, sep="\t")
    dim = df.shape[0]
    count = len(df.groupby("target")["target"].count())
    return f'Total IS: {dim}, Total unique IS: {count}'

def calc_seq(filepath: str):
    with open(filepath, "r") as file:
        records = list(SeqIO.parse(file, "fasta"))
    return(len(records))

# Filepath
folder = Path("../result/aft_sort/..").resolve()
for file in folder.rglob("*.tsv"):
    print(file.name)
    print(unique_is(file))

sorted_loci.tsv
Total IS: 74, Total unique IS: 50
unsorted_loci.tsv
Total IS: 5, Total unique IS: 5

It seems that the total number of unique IS found correspond to total number of loci created. If we back to the table from week 2, we found this:



Total IS
Complete
Simulated
Fragmented




Total rows
37

Total unique IS
4
35
24



However, after we sorted it we found: | Total IS | Complete (fa) | Complete (gfa) | Simulated (gfa)| |———|:—–|——|——:| | Total rows | 513 | 334 | 343 | | Total unique IS | 38 | 43 | 40 |


I compared the file _loci.fasta for the complete genome file. The one after sorting has more loci.
# calculate after sorting
grep "^>" result/aft_sort/comp_fa/eris_new_complete_loci.fasta | wc -l

# calculate before sorting
grep "^>" result/bfr_sort/com_fa/com_fa_loci.fasta | wc -l
The before sorting only have 4 loci assembled, while after sorting have 60 assembled loci
In the pipeline.py in extract_sequence function it mentioned that they are “Extracts the full nucleotide sequence of the locus from the assembly. Handles multi-contig loci by stitching segments together in graph order”.
I want to see how does Eris create the locus and the position they retained before and after sorting

import importlib
import eris.pipeline
from eris.pipeline import Pipeline
from eris.graph import TopologyEngine 
from eris.io import GenomeAssembly, TargetDatabase
import pandas as pd
importlib.reload(eris.pipeline)

# Target database
target_db = TargetDatabase("../../data/IS_n.mmi")

# Do genome assembly
genome = GenomeAssembly.from_file("../../data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta")

# See how does the locus builder actually work
def collect_loci(genome, target_db, label):
    rows = []
    with Pipeline(target_db=target_db) as pipeline:
        for locus in pipeline(genome):
            target = locus.targets[0] if locus.targets else None
            num_segments = len(target.segments) if target else 0
            rows.append({
                "label":        label,
                "locus_id":     locus.id,
                "target":       target.id if target else None,
                "num_segments": num_segments,
                "is_stitched":  num_segments > 1,
            })
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)

sort = collect_loci(genome, target_db, label="sorted")

sort.to_csv("../result/sorted_loci.tsv", sep="\t",index= False)

I used the same code, however, I changed the code inside pipeline.py manually (deleting/adding .sort())

In the pipeline.py in extract_sequence function it mentioned that they are “Extracts the full nucleotide sequence of the locus from the assembly. Handles multi-contig loci by stitching segments together in graph order”.
I want to see how does Eris create the locus and the position they retained before and after sorting

import importlib
import eris.pipeline
from eris.pipeline import Pipeline
from eris.graph import TopologyEngine 
from eris.io import GenomeAssembly, TargetDatabase
import pandas as pd
importlib.reload(eris.pipeline)

# Target database
target_db = TargetDatabase("../../data/IS_n.mmi")

# Do genome assembly
genome = GenomeAssembly.from_file("../../data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta")

# See how does the locus builder actually work
def collect_loci(genome, target_db, label):
    rows = []
    with Pipeline(target_db=target_db) as pipeline:
        for locus in pipeline(genome):
            target = locus.targets[0] if locus.targets else None
            num_segments = len(target.segments) if target else 0
            rows.append({
                "label":        label,
                "locus_id":     locus.id,
                "target":       target.id if target else None,
                "num_segments": num_segments,
                "is_stitched":  num_segments > 1,
            })
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)

sort = collect_loci(genome, target_db, label="sorted")

sort.to_csv("../result/sorted_loci.tsv", sep="\t",index= False)
